nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving streams, shallow waters, ditches, ponds, and oxbow lakes

summary: Cabomba is a long-established freshwater stem plant from South America, recognised by its soft, finely divided, feather-like leaves arranged in opposite pairs along brittle stems. It grows quickly under high light and benefits from CO2 supplementation, making it best suited to aquariums with stable, soft, slightly acidic water.
careNotes: Cabomba aquatica, commonly sold as Giant Cabomba or Yellow Cabomba, is a stem plant native to slow-moving freshwaters across South America. It produces delicate, feathery leaves that branch repeatedly into fine segments and are arranged in opposite pairs along the stem. Stems can reach 30–80 cm in length. A note on naming: the trade label 'Green Cabomba' is widely misapplied in the hobby and almost always refers to the closely related Cabomba caroliniana (Carolina Fanwort), which is a separate species. Cabomba furcata — sometimes called Red Cabomba or listed under the older name Cabomba piauhyensis — is a third distinct species. C. aquatica is the largest of the three. Growth rate under good conditions has been reported as fast, with stems extending roughly 2.5 cm per day. The stems are notably brittle, so it is generally advised to handle them gently when planting or trimming and to avoid disturbing established stands. The plant tends to be sensitive to sudden parameter swings and is prone to melting if water hardness, pH, or temperature changes abruptly. Keeping conditions stable around 22–26 °C in soft, slightly acidic water (pH approximately 6.5, gH 3–8) is broadly recommended. Cabomba aquatica is primarily a water-column feeder, meaning it draws most of its nutrients from the surrounding water rather than through its roots — as a result, liquid fertilisation tends to outperform root tabs alone, and substrate type is less critical than maintaining adequate column nutrients. For best appearance it is generally planted in dense groups in the midground or background.
breedingNotes: Propagated by terminal stem cuttings (4–12 cm); remove the lowest few centimetres of leaves and replant gently into the substrate. Cuttings root easily under stable conditions but are prone to damage if handled roughly. Lateral side-shoots from mature stems can also be separated and replanted.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied Tropica search URL was resolved to the species page at plantdetails/4431/4431. Tropica rates Cabomba aquatica as easy, medium light demand, low CO2 demand, fast growth.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, lighting, CO2, placement, and propagation. Buce Plant calls for moderate-to-high light and recommends CO2 for best growth; lists pH 6.0–7.5.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (aquariumbreeder.com)
additional.notes: Used for temperature (20–28 °C), gH (3–8), high-light requirement (~40–50 PAR), CO2 recommendation, fast growth (≈2.5 cm/day), and brittleness note. Rates care as moderate, which together with the manifest's high-light/CO2 guidance supported promoting careLevel to intermediate vs the easy rating from Tropica/Buce.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature range (68–82 °F), pH (6.0–7.5), maximum height ≈ 31 in (~80 cm), and South American native range.
